questions.solution: Copper was the first metal used by humans, leading to the Chalcolithic (Copper) Age before the Bronze Age. Copper occurs naturally in metallic form and was easy to work with. Students sometimes guess Iron as it is most common today.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Who wrote Ramcharita?
questions.solution: Ramcharita was written by Sandhyakara Nandi in the 12th century CE, describing the victories of the Pala king Ramapala. It is a Sanskrit text. Students confuse it with Ramcharitmanas (Tulsidas) which is in Awadhi.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Which statements are correct? I. Alexander invaded India in 326 BC. II. Ambhi of Taxila welcomed Alexander. III. Alexander died in 332 BC.
questions.optionA: Only I and III are correct.
questions.optionB: Only II and III are correct.
questions.optionD: Only I and II are correct.
questions.solution: Statements I and II are correct. Alexander died in 323 BC in Babylon, not 332 BC (Statement III is false). 332 BC was when Alexander conquered Egypt. Students must remember Alexander's death year precisely.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Which organisation was set up to check the impact of Christian missionaries?
questions.solution: Arya Samaj, founded by Dayananda Saraswati in 1875, was specifically established to counter the influence of Christian missionaries and revive Vedic religion. Brahmo Samaj focused on monotheism, not countering Christianity. Students confuse the objectives of these reform movements.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Who was the first secretary of Brahmo Sabha?
questions.solution: Tarachand Chakraborty was the first secretary of the Brahmo Sabha founded by Raja Ram Mohan Roy in 1828. Roy was the founder, not the secretary. Students often confuse the roles of founders and secretaries in reform organisations.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Baithun School, set up in 1849, was associated with
questions.solution: Baithun School (1849) was established in Bengal for the education of women. It was associated with reformers working for women's upliftment. Students may confuse it with schools for depressed classes established by other reformers.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Which statements are correct? 1. August Offer made in 1940. 2. Poona Pact between Gandhi and Ambedkar. 3. Third Round Table Conference held in 1932.
questions.solution: All three statements are correct: August Offer 1940 (by Viceroy Linlithgow), Poona Pact 1932 (Gandhi-Ambedkar on separate electorates), Third RTC 1932. Students often doubt Statement 3, thinking there were only two RTCs.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Which statements are correct? 1. GOI Act 1919 introduced dyarchy in provinces. 2. Act provided for direct election to Council of State and Legislative Assembly.
questions.solution: Both statements are correct: The Montagu-Chelmsford Act 1919 introduced dyarchy in provinces (Statement 1) and provided for direct elections to the Central Legislature (Statement 2). Students may doubt Statement 2, thinking direct elections came only with the 1935 Act.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Which statements are correct? 1. Raja Ram Mohan Roy — Father of Modern India. 2. Sayyid Ahmad Khan — Muslim communalism. 3. Bhagat Singh and Batukeshwar Dutt threw bombs in Central Assembly 1929.
questions.solution: All three statements are correct: Ram Mohan Roy is called Father of Modern India; Sayyid Ahmad Khan's Aligarh Movement is seen as fuelling Muslim separatism; Bhagat Singh and BK Dutt threw bombs in the Central Assembly on April 8, 1929. Students doubt Statement 3 thinking it was in Parliament.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Which statements about the Revolt of 1857 are correct? 1. Started by BEIC sepoys. 2. Queen Victoria's proclamation 1858 led to complete integration of armies. 3. Major turning point in history.
questions.solution: Statements 1 and 3 are correct. Statement 2 is incorrect: Queen Victoria's proclamation (1858) transferred power to the Crown but did NOT lead to complete integration of British and Indian armies; they remained separate. Students assume the proclamation unified all aspects of administration.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Which statements are correct? 1. Young Bengal Movement founded by H.V. Derozio. 2. Vidyasagar campaigned for widow remarriage. 3. Brahmo Samaj denounced polytheism and idol worship.
questions.solution: All three statements are correct. Derozio founded Young Bengal; Vidyasagar was instrumental in the Hindu Widow's Remarriage Act 1856; Brahmo Samaj (founded by Ram Mohan Roy) rejected idol worship and polytheism. Students may doubt Statement 3's specificity about Brahmo Samaj.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Which statements are correct? 1. Chengiz Khan reached Indus during Iltutmish's reign. 2. Taimur crossed Indus during Muhammad bin Tughlaq's reign. 3. Vasco da Gama reached Kerala during Deva Raya II's reign.
questions.solution: Only Statement 1 is correct: Chengiz Khan pursued the Khwarezm prince to the Indus during Iltutmish's reign (1221). Statement 2 is wrong — Taimur invaded during Nasiruddin Mahmud Shah Tughluq's reign (1398). Statement 3 is wrong — Vasco da Gama arrived in 1498 during Saluva Narasimha's or early Tuluva period. Students conflate different medieval rulers.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Which statements about Alauddin Khilji's administration are correct? 1. Market control policy. 2. Silver tanka currency for price control. 3. First sultan to fix land revenue in cash. 4. Constructed Siri Fort.
questions.solution: Statements 1, 3, and 4 are correct. Statement 2 is incorrect — the silver tanka was introduced by Iltutmish, not Alauddin Khilji. Alauddin's price control used market superintendents and spies, not a new currency. Students attribute all Khilji reforms to Alauddin.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Which statements about Emperor Akbar are correct? 1. Issued Mahzarnama. 2. Mansabdari system primarily determined salary. 3. Battle of Haldighati 1576 was decisive Mughal victory.
questions.solution: Statements 1 and 2 are correct. Statement 3 is partially wrong — Haldighati (1576) was NOT a decisive victory; Maharana Pratap escaped and continued resistance. Akbar's forces won the battle but couldn't capture Pratap. Students think any battle won by Mughals was 'decisive'.

questions.subject: Others
questions.question: The Planning Commission was established in the year
questions.solution: The Planning Commission of India was established on March 15, 1950. It was replaced by NITI Aayog in 2015. Students confuse the year of independence (1947) with the establishment of planning institutions.

questions.subject: Others
questions.question: The very first five-year plan of India was based on the model of:
questions.solution: India's First Five Year Plan (1951-56) was based on the Harrod-Domar growth model focusing on savings and investment. The Mahalanobis model was used in the Second Five Year Plan. Students commonly confuse the two models.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: Which of the following pair is not correctly matched?
questions.optionA: Total area of India - 3.28 million sq. km
questions.solution: India currently has 28 states (after Jammu & Kashmir was bifurcated into 2 UTs in 2019). The number 29 is outdated. All other options are correct. Students may not be updated on the reorganisation of J&K.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: Which of the following rivers of India crosses the tropic of cancer twice?
questions.solution: The Mahi River in Gujarat/Rajasthan crosses the Tropic of Cancer twice due to its curved course. The Narmada and Chambal flow roughly parallel to the Tropic of Cancer but do not cross it twice. Students rarely know the specific course of the Mahi river.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: Which statement(s) about the Indian desert is/are correct? (a) Lies towards western margins of Aravalli Hills (b) Rainfall below 150 mm per year (c) Luni is only large river (d) All the above
questions.optionA: Lies towards western margins of Aravalli Hills
questions.optionC: Luni is the only large river in this region
questions.solution: All three statements about the Thar (Indian) Desert are correct: it lies west of the Aravallis, receives less than 150 mm rainfall, and the Luni is the only significant river. Students may doubt the rainfall figure or the Aravalli direction.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: The correct south to northward sequence of Zaskar, Dhauladhar, Ladakh, Karakoram ranges is:
questions.solution: From south to north: Dhauladhar (2) → Zaskar (1) → Ladakh (3) → Karakoram (4). This is the standard sequence of Himalayan ranges from the Shivaliks northward. Students often confuse Zaskar and Ladakh range positions.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: Which statement is NOT correct about Indian geography?
questions.optionA: The Western Ghats are relatively high in their Northern region.
questions.optionB: The Anaimudi is the highest peak in the Western Ghats.
questions.optionC: Tapti river lies to the south of Satpura.
questions.optionD: The Narmada and the Tapti river valleys are said to be old rift valleys.
questions.solution: The Western Ghats are higher in the SOUTH (Anaimudi, 2695m) and lower in the north. Statement (a) says they are high in the northern region, which is incorrect. All other options are factually accurate. Students confuse north-south elevation patterns.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: Which statements about Barren Island volcano are correct? 1. Active volcano in Indian territory. 2. Lies ~140 km east of Great Nicobar. 3. Last eruption in 1991, inactive since.
questions.solution: Only Statement 1 is correct: Barren Island is the only confirmed active volcano in South Asia, located in the Andaman Sea. Statement 2 is wrong — it is about 135 km northeast of Port Blair, not east of Great Nicobar. Statement 3 is wrong — it has erupted multiple times after 1991, including in 2017 and 2020.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: Toda Tribe is the inhabitant of:
questions.solution: The Toda tribe is an indigenous pastoral community inhabiting the Nilgiri Hills of Tamil Nadu. They are known for their distinct culture and circular thatched huts. Students may confuse them with tribes from central Indian hill ranges.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: Assertion: Yamuna becomes a dead river between Delhi and Agra most of the year. Reason: Yamuna is a non-perennial river.
questions.optionA: Both (A) and (R) are true, and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).
questions.optionB: Both (A) and (R) are true, but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A).
questions.solution: The Assertion is correct — Yamuna is effectively 'dead' (highly polluted, no fresh flow) between Delhi and Agra due to industrial effluents. The Reason is FALSE — Yamuna IS a perennial river fed by Himalayan glaciers. The real reason is pollution and water diversion, not non-perennial nature.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: The Indian monsoon is indicated by seasonal displacement because of
questions.optionA: Differential temperature of land and sea
questions.solution: The Indian monsoon is driven by the differential heating of land and sea (land heats and cools faster than sea), creating pressure differences that cause seasonal wind reversal. This thermal gradient is the primary cause. Students may overcomplicate this with ITCZ theory.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: Sargasso Sea is related to the Ocean, that ocean is –
questions.solution: The Sargasso Sea is a region of the North Atlantic Ocean, bounded by four ocean currents forming the North Atlantic Gyre. It is named after the Sargassum seaweed that floats on its surface. Students confuse it with the Pacific Ocean.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: Which of the following is a warm current?
questions.solution: The Brazil Current is a warm ocean current flowing southward along the eastern coast of South America. Falkland and Labrador are cold currents; Canary is also a cold current off NW Africa. Students often confuse Atlantic Ocean currents.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: Which strait connects the Red Sea and the Indian Ocean?
questions.solution: The Strait of Bab-el-Mandeb connects the Red Sea to the Gulf of Aden and the Indian Ocean. The Strait of Hormuz connects the Persian Gulf to the Gulf of Oman. Students often confuse these two strategically important straits.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: 'Great Barrier Reef' is—
questions.optionD: A range of the Rocky Mountains
questions.solution: The Great Barrier Reef is the world's largest coral reef system, located off the coast of Queensland, Australia. It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Options (b), (c), (d) are obviously incorrect distractors.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: Arrange atmospheric zones in increasing order of distance from earth's surface: 1.Thermosphere 2.Troposphere 4.Mesosphere 3.Stratosphere
questions.solution: The correct order from Earth's surface (increasing distance): Troposphere(2) → Stratosphere(3) → Mesosphere(4) → Thermosphere(1). The mnemonic 'The Stratosphere Meets Thermos' helps remember. Students often place Mesosphere and Thermosphere in wrong order.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: A sudden fall of Mercury in Barometer indicates;
questions.solution: A sudden fall in barometric pressure (mercury falling rapidly) indicates an approaching storm or bad weather. High pressure indicates fair weather; low and falling pressure signals storms. Students confuse gradual fall (rain) with sudden fall (storm).

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: The headquarter of the International Maritime Organization is located at:
questions.solution: The International Maritime Organization (IMO) is headquartered in London, UK. Geneva hosts WHO, WTO, and other UN bodies. Students confuse London-based international organisations with Geneva-based ones.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following will contain same number of atoms as 20 g of calcium?
questions.solution: 20 g Ca = \(\frac{20}{40}\) = 0.5 mol Ca = 0.5 mol atoms. 12 g C = \(\frac{12}{12}\) = 1 mol C? No — 0.5 mol Ca needs 0.5 mol atoms of another element. \(\frac{12}{12}\times N_A = N_A\) atoms (1 mol), not same. Actually 20/40 = 0.5 mol Ca. For same atoms: 0.5 mol C = 6 g, 0.5 mol Mg = 12 g. Answer is (b) 12 g of carbon (0.5 mol × 12 = 6g)... re-examining: 12g carbon = 1 mol = \(6.02\times10^{23}\) atoms; 20g Ca = 0.5 mol = \(3.01\times10^{23}\) atoms. The matching option should give 0.5 mol. 24g Mg = 1 mol Mg atoms ≠. 12g C = 1 mol ≠. Students must equate moles of atoms correctly.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A device used for measurement of radioactivity is
questions.solution: The Geiger-Müller (G.M.) counter detects and measures ionising radiation (alpha, beta, gamma). A mass spectrometer separates ions by mass; a cyclotron accelerates particles. Students may confuse instruments used in nuclear physics.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Molten sodium chloride conducts electricity due to the presence of
questions.solution: Molten NaCl dissociates into Na\(^+\) and Cl\(^-\) ions which carry electrical charge. Free electrons conduct electricity in metals, not ionic compounds. Students confuse metallic conduction (electrons) with electrolytic conduction (ions).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Balmer series in hydrogen spectrum appears in
questions.solution: The Balmer series corresponds to electron transitions to n=2 level in hydrogen, emitting visible light (wavelengths 400–700 nm). Lyman series is UV; Paschen/Brackett are infrared. Students confuse the series with their spectral regions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In Rutherford's experiment, when thin metal foils were bombarded with \(\alpha\)-particles:
questions.optionA: Most alpha particles pass through without deflection
questions.optionB: All alpha particles pass through the foil
questions.optionC: Most alpha particles are deflected through small angles
questions.optionD: Most alpha particles are deflected back
questions.solution: Most \(\alpha\)-particles passed straight through because the atom is mostly empty space. Only a few were deflected at large angles due to the dense, positive nucleus. Students confuse the result with the conclusion (nuclear model) and think most are deflected.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Select the correct statement from the following
questions.optionA: Bronze is an alloy of copper and tin
questions.optionB: Brass is an alloy of copper and aluminium
questions.optionD: Stainless steel is chromium plated iron
questions.solution: Bronze = Cu + Sn (copper and tin). Brass = Cu + Zn (copper and zinc, not aluminium). German silver contains Cu, Zn, Ni — no actual silver. Stainless steel is an alloy (Fe + Cr + Ni), not chromium plated. Students confuse brass and bronze compositions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Electrical conductivity of metal decreases
questions.solution: In metals, conductivity decreases with increasing temperature because lattice vibrations increase, causing more collisions with electrons. This is opposite to semiconductors (whose conductivity increases with temperature). Students confuse metal and semiconductor behaviour.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Kirchhoff's current law is based on
questions.solution: KCL states that the sum of currents entering a node equals the sum leaving it, which is based on conservation of electric charge. KVL (voltage law) is based on conservation of energy. Students swap the bases of KCL and KVL.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The dimensions of heat capacity are
questions.solution: Heat capacity = \(\frac{\text{Heat energy}}{\text{Temperature}}\). Heat energy has dimensions \([ML^2T^{-2}]\) and temperature has dimension \([K]\). So heat capacity = \([ML^2T^{-2}K^{-1}]\). Students often forget to take the reciprocal of K.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A black body is at 500 K. It emits energy at a rate proportional to
questions.solution: By Stefan-Boltzmann law, power emitted by a black body \(E \propto T^4\). At T = 500 K, \(E \propto (500)^4\). Students sometimes confuse with Wien's law or think the power is proportional to T only.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: \(n\) equal resistors are first connected in series and then connected in parallel. What is the ratio of the maximum resistance to the minimum resistance?
questions.solution: Series resistance \(R_s = nR\) (maximum); Parallel resistance \(R_p = \frac{R}{n}\) (minimum). Ratio = \(\frac{nR}{R/n} = n^2\). Students forget to divide both and often calculate just nR/R = n.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The ratio of the dimension of Planck's constant and that of moment of inertia is the dimension of
questions.solution: Planck's constant \(h\) has dimensions \([ML^2T^{-1}]\). Moment of inertia has dimensions \([ML^2]\). Ratio = \(\frac{[ML^2T^{-1}]}{[ML^2]} = [T^{-1}]\) = frequency. Students may guess velocity by intuition without computing dimensions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A bullet of mass 2 gm has charge \(2\mu C\). Through what potential difference must it be accelerated from rest to acquire speed of 10 m/s?
questions.solution: Work-energy theorem: \(qV = \frac{1}{2}mv^2\). So \(V = \frac{mv^2}{2q} = \frac{(2\times10^{-3})(10)^2}{2\times(2\times10^{-6})} = \frac{0.2}{4\times10^{-6}} = 50000\) V = 50 kV. The correct answer should be (c) 50 kV. Students must be careful with unit conversions (gm to kg, \(\mu C\) to C).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Three resistances each of \(4\Omega\) are connected to form a triangle. The resistance between any two terminals is
questions.solution: Between any two terminals: one arm (\(4\Omega\)) is in parallel with two arms in series (\(8\Omega\)). Equivalent resistance = \(\frac{4\times8}{4+8} = \frac{32}{12} = \frac{8}{3}\Omega\). Students forget that two sides are in series and that combination is in parallel with the third side.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Bernoulli's theorem is based upon
questions.solution: Bernoulli's theorem is derived from the work-energy theorem and represents conservation of energy in fluid flow (pressure energy + kinetic energy + potential energy = constant). Continuity equation is based on mass conservation. Students sometimes say momentum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An iron needle floats on the surface of water. This phenomenon is attributed to:
questions.solution: A needle floats due to surface tension — the water surface behaves like an elastic membrane supporting the needle. Upthrust (buoyancy) would be insufficient for a denser-than-water object. Students often guess upthrust thinking about Archimedes' principle.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Chemical formula of borax (Sodium tetraborate) is
questions.solution: Borax is \(Na_2B_4O_7 \cdot 10H_2O\) (sodium tetraborate decahydrate). It contains 10 molecules of water of crystallisation. Students confuse it with washing soda (\(Na_2CO_3 \cdot 10H_2O\)) or confuse the number of water molecules.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A moving body is covering distances in proportion to the square of time. The acceleration of the body is
questions.solution: If \(s \propto t^2\), then \(s = kt^2\) for some constant k. Differentiating: \(v = 2kt\) and \(a = 2k\) = constant. This is the equation of uniformly accelerated motion. Students may think acceleration increases because velocity increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A convex lens has focal length \(f\). It makes a virtual image of size \(n\) times the object. The distance of the object from the lens is
questions.solution: For virtual image by convex lens: magnification \(m = n = \frac{v}{u}\) and lens formula \(\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u}\). Using \(v = nu\): \(\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{nu} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1-n}{nu}\), so \(u = \frac{(1-n)f}{n} = \frac{(n-1)}{n}f\) (taking magnitude). Students confuse sign conventions for virtual images.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The purest form of Fe is
questions.solution: Wrought iron is the purest form of iron with carbon content less than 0.05%. Cast iron has 2-4% carbon; steel has 0.2-2% carbon; stainless steel contains chromium and nickel. Students confuse purity with strength.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: According to which law CO and \(CO_2\) are formed?
questions.solution: The Law of Multiple Proportions states that when two elements (C and O) combine to form more than one compound (CO and \(CO_2\)), the masses of one element that combine with a fixed mass of the other are in simple whole number ratios (1:2 for oxygen). Students confuse this with the law of constant proportions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The neutron was discovered by.
questions.solution: The neutron was discovered by James Chadwick in 1932. Rutherford discovered the proton; Goldstein discovered canal rays (protons); Moseley worked on atomic numbers and X-ray spectra. Students confuse the discoverers of subatomic particles.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following oxides is acidic?
questions.solution: \(SO_2\) is an acidic oxide (dissolves in water to form \(H_2SO_3\)). \(Na_2O\) is a basic oxide. \(Al_2O_3\) is amphoteric (both acidic and basic). Students may choose \(Al_2O_3\) because it can show acidic properties, but it is primarily amphoteric.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The large energy released in atomic bomb explosion is due to
questions.optionC: conversion of heavy atoms to lighter atoms
questions.solution: The energy released in nuclear fission is due to mass defect — a small amount of mass (\(\Delta m\)) is converted to energy as per \(E = \Delta mc^2\). This 'appreciable loss of mass' results in enormous energy. Students think it is due to neutron release alone.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following is amphoteric?
questions.solution: \(ZnO\) is amphoteric — it reacts with both acids (\(ZnO + H_2SO_4 \to ZnSO_4 + H_2O\)) and bases (\(ZnO + NaOH \to Na_2ZnO_2 + H_2O\)). \(Li_2O\) is basic; \(SO_2\) is acidic. Students may not know ZnO's amphoteric nature.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The energy equivalent to 1 kilogram of matter is about
questions.solution: By \(E = mc^2\): \(E = 1 \times (3\times10^8)^2 = 9\times10^{16} \approx 10^{17}\) joule. The speed of light \(c = 3\times10^8\) m/s. Students forget to square c and underestimate the result.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following phenomena cannot take place in sound waves?
questions.solution: Polarisation can only occur in transverse waves (like light). Sound waves are longitudinal (compressions and rarefactions) and CANNOT be polarised. Reflection, interference, and diffraction are exhibited by both transverse and longitudinal waves.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The number of electrons contained in 1 coulomb of charge is
questions.solution: Charge of one electron \(e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\) C. Number of electrons in 1 C = \(\frac{1}{1.6\times10^{-19}} = 6.25 \times 10^{18}\). Students confuse the value of e with its reciprocal and misplace the power.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Refractive indices of glass and water w.r.t. air are \(\frac{3}{2}\) and \(\frac{4}{3}\) respectively. The refractive index of glass w.r.t. water will be
questions.solution: \(n_{gw} = \frac{n_{ga}}{n_{wa}} = \frac{3/2}{4/3} = \frac{3}{2} \times \frac{3}{4} = \frac{9}{8}\). The refractive index of glass with respect to water = \(\frac{9}{8}\). Students often invert the ratio or confuse which value goes in the numerator.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: When a monochromatic light passes from vacuum to a material, which characteristic does NOT change?
questions.solution: Frequency of light does NOT change when it passes from one medium to another. Velocity (\(v = c/n\)) and wavelength (\(\lambda = \lambda_0/n\)) both change with medium. Students confuse wavelength and frequency changes in different media.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two wires of same metal, same length, cross-sections in ratio 3:1, joined in series. Resistance of thicker wire = \(10\Omega\). Total resistance of combination:
questions.solution: Resistance \(R \propto \frac{1}{A}\). Thicker wire (area 3A): \(R_1 = 10\Omega\). Thinner wire (area A): \(R_2 = 10 \times 3 = 30\Omega\). Total in series: \(R = 10 + 30 = 40\Omega\). Students use ratio in wrong direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The time period of a simple pendulum is 2 seconds. If its length is increased by 4 times, then its period becomes
questions.solution: \(T \propto \sqrt{L}\). If L is increased 4 times: \(T' = T\sqrt{4} = 2 \times 2 = 4\) seconds. Students sometimes think T increases by 4 (getting 8 s) by forgetting the square root relationship.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The temperature at which speed of sound becomes double that at 27°C is
questions.solution: Speed of sound \(v \propto \sqrt{T}\) (in Kelvin). At 27°C = 300 K. For double speed: \(\frac{v'}{v} = \sqrt{\frac{T'}{300}} = 2\), so \(T' = 4 \times 300 = 1200\) K = 927°C. Students forget to convert to Kelvin and work directly in Celsius.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: At 0 K, which of the following properties of a gas will be zero?
questions.solution: At absolute zero (0 K), all molecular motion ceases and the kinetic energy of gas molecules becomes zero. Mass and density do not become zero; potential energy depends on intermolecular forces, not temperature alone. Students confuse 0°C with 0 K.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Time taken by sunlight to pass through a glass window of thickness 4 mm with refractive index \(\frac{3}{2}\)
questions.solution: Speed in glass \(v = \frac{c}{n} = \frac{3\times10^8}{1.5} = 2\times10^8\) m/s. Time \(t = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{4\times10^{-3}}{2\times10^8} = 2\times10^{-11}\) s. Students forget to divide c by n and use c directly.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A 2 kg and 4 kg ball are dropped from 60 feet. After falling 30 feet, their respective kinetic energies ratio:
questions.solution: KE = mgh (same height h = 30 ft for both). KE\(_1 = 2gh\), KE\(_2 = 4gh\). Ratio = \(\frac{2}{4} = 1:2\). Both experience same acceleration (g), so ratio of KE = ratio of masses. Students may think heavier ball falls faster.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Superconductivity observed at 95K. This temperature is approximately equivalent to
questions.solution: 95 K = 95 - 273 = -178°C. To convert to Fahrenheit: \(F = -178 \times \frac{9}{5} + 32 = -320.4 + 32 = -288.4°F \approx -288°F\). Actually option (c) -288°F matches better. Rechecking: 95K = -178°C = (-178×9/5)+32 = -288.4°F ≈ -288°F. Students must use the correct conversion formula.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: When a wire is stretched and its radius becomes \(\frac{r}{2}\), its resistance will be
questions.solution: Volume constant: \(\pi r^2 L = \pi (r/2)^2 L'\) → \(L' = 4L\). New resistance \(R' = \rho \frac{L'}{A'} = \rho \frac{4L}{\pi(r/2)^2} = \rho \frac{4L}{\pi r^2/4} = 16\rho\frac{L}{\pi r^2} = 16R\). Students forget volume is conserved when stretching.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The temperature of sun can be found out by using
questions.solution: Wien's Displacement Law (\(\lambda_{max} T = b\)) relates the peak wavelength of emitted radiation to temperature, allowing calculation of the Sun's surface temperature from its emission spectrum. Stefan-Boltzmann law gives total power. Students confuse the two laws' applications.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following statements is NOT true?
questions.optionA: Resistance of intrinsic semiconductors decreases with increase of temperature.
questions.optionB: Doping pure Si with trivalent impurity gives p-type semiconductors.
questions.optionC: The majority carriers in n-type semiconductors are holes.
questions.optionD: A p-n junction can act as a semiconductor diode.
questions.solution: In n-type semiconductors, the majority carriers are ELECTRONS (not holes). Holes are majority carriers in p-type semiconductors. Statement (c) is false and is the answer. Statements (a), (b), (d) are all correct facts about semiconductors.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The vitamins, which are generally excreted in urine are
questions.solution: Water-soluble vitamins (B-complex and C) are excreted in urine when in excess. Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) are stored in body fat and liver. Students confuse fat-soluble with water-soluble vitamins.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Deficiency of which elements is responsible for weakening of bones? 1.Calcium 2.Phosphorus 3.Nitrogen 4.Carbon
questions.solution: Calcium and Phosphorus are the primary minerals in bones (as hydroxyapatite). Deficiency of either weakens bones. Nitrogen and Carbon are structural elements in organic molecules but not specifically responsible for bone weakness. Students may include more elements than necessary.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which one among the following animal tissues transports hormones and maintains water balance?
questions.solution: Connective tissue, specifically blood (a fluid connective tissue), transports hormones and helps maintain water balance (osmotic balance). Areolar tissue is loose connective tissue providing support. Students may confuse blood with nervous tissue for transport.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following structures of a plant is responsible for transpiration?
questions.solution: Transpiration (loss of water vapour from plants) primarily occurs through stomata on leaves. Xylem transports water from roots; phloem transports food. Students confuse the structure for water transport (xylem) with that for transpiration (stomata).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Cinnabar is an ore of
questions.solution: Cinnabar (HgS) is the principal ore of mercury (Hg). Copper ore includes chalcopyrite; nickel comes from pentlandite; tungsten from wolframite. Students confuse cinnabar with copper ores due to its reddish colour.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Wavelengths of visible light \(\lambda_v\), X-rays \(\lambda_x\) and microwaves \(\lambda_m\): correct order is
questions.solution: In the EM spectrum: microwaves have the longest wavelength (\(\sim\)mm–cm), visible light intermediate (\(\sim\)400–700 nm), X-rays very short (\(\sim\)0.01–10 nm). So \(\lambda_m > \lambda_v > \lambda_x\). Students may misplace visible light and X-rays in the spectrum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An object weighs 10N in air. When fully immersed in liquid, it weighs 8N. The weight of liquid displaced is
questions.solution: By Archimedes' principle, buoyant force = weight in air − weight in liquid = 10 − 8 = 2N. The buoyant force equals the weight of liquid displaced = 2N. Students sometimes calculate the weight of liquid displaced as the weight in liquid (8N).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Source of sun's energy is
questions.solution: The sun produces energy through nuclear fusion — hydrogen nuclei fuse to form helium, releasing enormous energy (\(4H \to He + energy\)). Fission (splitting heavy nuclei) is used in nuclear reactors and atomic bombs, not in stars. Students confuse fusion and fission.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two resistances of \(10\Omega\) are parallelly connected. Equivalent resistance will be
questions.solution: For two equal resistors R in parallel: \(R_{eq} = \frac{R}{2} = \frac{10}{2} = 5\Omega\). Students may add them (series) or use the wrong formula. The parallel formula is \(\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}\).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: "Good emitters are good absorbers." This statement is based on
questions.solution: Kirchhoff's Law of radiation states that at thermal equilibrium, good emitters are also good absorbers of radiation. Stefan's law gives total radiated power; Newton's law relates cooling rate to temperature difference. Students confuse Kirchhoff's radiation law with his electrical circuit laws.
